 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.4","title":"Switch default CLI, TUI, and robot search intent to hybrid-preferred behavior","description":"BACKGROUND:
Today cass presents lexical mode as the default search mode. That leaks an implementation detail into the product contract and prevents agents from benefiting from semantic refinement automatically. The user requirement is that hybrid behavior should be the default expectation, while still preserving lexical immediacy and fail-open reliability.

GOAL:
Change cass so the default search intent across CLI, TUI, and robot consumers is hybrid-preferred rather than lexical-first-from-the-user's-perspective.

SCOPE:
- Audit default mode selection in CLI parsing, TUI interactions, robot/help text, capabilities docs, and any config/env defaults.
- Change default intent to hybrid-preferred in user-facing surfaces.
- Preserve explicit override flags for lexical-only and semantic-only modes.
- Ensure metadata clearly reports when a command used lexical-only fallback instead of full hybrid refinement.
- Update examples and help so future agents naturally use the intended path.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- "Default hybrid" must describe user intent, not a brittle hard requirement that semantic assets be fully present.
- This bead should not itself implement fail-open semantics; it should change defaults and surfaces so the runtime planner bead can honor the new contract consistently.
- Any existing language saying lexical is the default should be updated or removed to avoid contradictory operator expectations.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Tests proving the effective mode for ordinary search commands is hybrid-preferred unless explicitly overridden.
- Tests proving explicit lexical mode still remains available and deterministic.
- Snapshot/help tests verifying the documented defaults match the implementation.

DONE WHEN:
A new agent reading cass help or using cass search without extra flags is guided onto the hybrid-preferred path by default, without sacrificing the ability to request lexical-only behavior explicitly.","design":"CONFIG SURFACE REFINEMENT:
- The default-hybrid switch must be implemented through the cohesive config/override layer so users can inspect and override the effective default intentionally rather than relying on hidden parser behavior.","acceptance_criteria":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- The effective default search intent across CLI, TUI, and robot surfaces is hybrid-preferred unless explicitly overridden, and that effective default is visible through the configuration or status surfaces rather than hidden parser behavior.
- Explicit lexical-only and semantic-only overrides remain deterministic, and user-visible metadata truthfully reports requested intent, realized path, fallback tier, and whether semantic refinement actually occurred.
- Unit, integration, help-snapshot, and CLI or robot E2E scenarios prove default intent, override behavior, and documented examples all stay aligned with the verified product contract.","notes":"TEST POLICY ADDENDUM: This bead must land with bead-local validation, not just final-epic validation. At minimum include targeted unit tests for the new invariants, integration tests for the affected runtime path, and at least one CLI/robot/E2E script when the behavior is user-visible or multi-step.  {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.5","title":"Add semantic asset manifest, backlog ledger, and resumable checkpoints","description":"BACKGROUND:
The semantic side of cass is currently too binary: either vector assets exist in just the right place or hybrid/semantic search fails. That is inadequate for a large evolving corpus. Semantic indexing needs the same first-class asset accounting that lexical indexing will get, but with optional semantics: partial availability should drive background backfill and truthful runtime degradation, not a confusing all-or-nothing user experience.

GOAL:
Introduce authoritative semantic asset manifests and backlog/checkpoint tracking so cass can answer exactly what semantic work remains, resume interrupted work, and know whether fast tier, quality tier, and ANN accelerators are ready for use.

SCOPE:
- Define manifest/state for each semantic artifact class: fast tier vectors, quality tier vectors, ANN/HNSW accelerators, and any future semantic derivatives.
- Fingerprint these assets against the canonical SQLite corpus/version and relevant model/schema versions.
- Track backlog/progress durably at the right grain (conversation, message batch, or chunk range) so interrupted work can resume without restarting the whole corpus.
- Support partial semantic readiness, e.g. fast tier ready while quality tier still backfilling.
- Ensure publish/swap semantics are crash-safe and never advertise an asset as ready before its manifest says so.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- The backlog ledger must support very large corpora without exploding metadata size.
- The design should support both full builds and incremental catch-up after new SQLite content lands.
- The state model must make it obvious whether hybrid refinement can run now, whether only fast tier can run, or whether lexical-only fallback is required.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Unit tests for manifest classification and partial readiness.
- Integration tests for interrupted semantic builds resuming from checkpoints.
- Tests for corpus/model/schema fingerprint mismatch forcing backfill without mislabeling assets as ready.

DONE WHEN:
Semantic indexing has an authoritative durable state model that tells cass exactly what semantic assets exist, how trustworthy they are, and what work remains to converge the corpus.","design":"UPGRADE AND MIGRATION REFINEMENTS:
- The semantic manifest layer must account for legacy pre-manifest semantic assets. If an existing asset is compatible, adopt it explicitly into the new state model; if incompatible, quarantine or invalidate it with a precise reason.
- Never allow mixed-fingerprint, mixed-schema, or partially adopted semantic assets to masquerade as one ready tier.","notes":"TEST POLICY ADDENDUM: This bead must land with bead-local validation, not just final-epic validation. At minimum include targeted unit tests for the new invariants, integration tests for the affected runtime path, and at least one CLI/robot/E2E script when the behavior is user-visible or multi-step. Test output must include rich, structured, timestamped logging and artifact snapshots sufficient for a future agent to diagnose failures without rerunning the entire scenario manually. Prefer extending the shared harness from coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.15 where applicable.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:17:34.634368285Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T18:21:13.817391643Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T18:21:13.817095148Z","close_reason":"Implemented src/search/semantic_manifest.rs with full durable manifest (SemanticManifest), per-tier ArtifactRecord, HnswRecord, BacklogLedger, BuildCheckpoint, TierReadiness classification, atomic save/load, legacy adoption, invalidation, and 24 table-driven unit tests covering round-trips, tier readiness, backlog accounting, checkpoint validation, publish/clear, invalidation, and JSON serialization.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backfill","indexing","semantic","state-model","vector"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.5","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:17:34.634368285Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.5","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:19:32.520070731Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.5","depends_on_id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.16","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:28:14.003837359Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.6","title":"Implement fail-open hybrid planner with lexical-first progressive refinement","description":"BACKGROUND:
Right now hybrid/semantic search can hard-fail when vector assets are missing, even though lexical search remains healthy. That violates the desired product contract. The runtime planner must treat semantic enrichment as opportunistic: it should improve results when available but never break ordinary search when absent, stale, or temporarily unavailable.

GOAL:
Make the default hybrid runtime path lexical-first and fail-open. Ordinary searches should return lexical results immediately, then add or upgrade semantic refinement only when the semantic state contract says it is safe and useful to do so.

SCOPE:
- Build a single runtime planner that consumes the state models from beads .1 and .5.
- For hybrid-preferred searches, return lexical results immediately when lexical is ready.
- Attempt semantic refinement only when the relevant semantic assets are ready enough.
- If semantic assets are missing, stale, partially built, temporarily locked, or erroring, complete the search lexically and report truthful metadata rather than surfacing a hard search failure.
- Define strict behavior for explicit semantic-only requests, including whether they retain hard-fail semantics or gain an opt-in strictness flag.
- Ensure TUI, CLI, and robot flows all use the same fail-open behavior.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- Lexical-first means user-visible latency stays predictable while semantic refinement remains additive.
- The planner must never silently claim hybrid quality when only lexical ranking was used; metadata and status must expose the truth.
- Partial semantic availability should still be useful: e.g. fast tier may refine even when quality tier is still backfilling.
- A transient semantic subsystem error should not poison subsequent lexical-only searches.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Integration tests where vector assets are fully absent, partially present, stale, or locked.
- Tests proving ordinary hybrid-preferred searches still return successful lexical hits in those cases.
- Tests proving refinement upgrades results when semantic assets become available.
- Tests for explicit semantic-only behavior so strict vs fail-open semantics remain intentional, not accidental.

DONE WHEN:
An agent can issue an ordinary search and trust that cass will never throw away usable lexical search just because semantic refinement is not ready yet.","design":"ORCHESTRATION REFINEMENT:
- The fail-open planner should consume orchestration/progress state when deciding whether to wait briefly, attach to a running maintenance task, or return lexical-only results immediately.","acceptance_criteria":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Ordinary hybrid-preferred searches return lexical results immediately whenever lexical search is healthy, then apply semantic refinement only when assets are ready enough and policy allows.
- The planner consumes orchestration and readiness truth when deciding whether to attach, wait boundedly, refine immediately, or complete lexically, and metadata truthfully reports the decision, realized refinement tier, stable result identity or digest continuity where useful, and any fail-open reason.
- Unit, integration, and CLI or robot E2E scenarios cover absent, partial, stale, locked, erroring, and newly available semantic assets, plus explicit semantic-only requests and progressive refinement without misleading result churn, with detailed structured logs and result-digest evidence.","notes":"TEST POLICY ADDENDUM: This bead must land with bead-local validation, not just final-epic validation. At minimum include targeted unit tests for the new invariants, integration tests for the affected runtime path, and at least one CLI/robot/E2E script when the behavior is user-visible or multi-step. Test output must include rich, structured, timestamped logging and artifact snapshots sufficient for a future agent to diagnose failures without rerunning the entire scenario manually. Prefer extending the shared harness from coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.15 where applicable.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:17:49.721989891Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T23:09:19.490169308Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T23:09:19.489893942Z","close_reason":"Hybrid search now fails open to lexical for default and explicit hybrid intent while semantic-only remains strict; - Added a formal acceptance bar for planner decisions because this is where the user contract is actually honored or broken at query time.
- The planner now explicitly owns reporting the decision path: immediate lexical, bounded wait, attach, semantic refinement tier, or fail-open reason.","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:48:05Z"}]}
-{"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.7","title":"Schedule low-impact background semantic backfill using idle and load budgets","description":"BACKGROUND:
The user requirement is not merely to support semantic indexing, but to do it without harming the machine's primary job: coding, search, and interactive use. That means semantic work must be opportunistic and budgeted. cass should quietly chew through vector work when the machine is idle or when work can be done with little impact, then yield quickly when foreground pressure returns. The scheduler is therefore a control layer over an already-correct worker/publish pipeline, not the place where actual semantic backfill correctness is invented.

GOAL:
Introduce a background scheduler that drives the existing semantic worker only when operating conditions are favorable and within explicit CPU/IO/latency budgets.

SCOPE:
- Define what cass treats as favorable background conditions: low foreground search pressure, no active lexical repair contention, acceptable CPU/load, acceptable IO pressure, and any available heuristics for user activity or interactive contention.
- Drive the worker from bead .8 rather than re-implementing semantic generation logic inside the scheduler.
- Implement pause/resume behavior so semantic work yields when the machine becomes busy again.
- Choose conservative concurrency and batching defaults.
- Support operator overrides and kill switches so future debugging or benchmarking can disable background behavior cleanly.
- Ensure the scheduler integrates with the semantic backlog ledger from bead .5 instead of inventing its own notion of remaining work.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- A scheduler that is too aggressive will destroy trust even if it technically works.
- Background work should prefer incremental progress over large monolithic jobs that take too long to pause.
- Scheduling decisions should be inspectable in logs/status so future agents can understand why work is paused or running.
- The implementation should leave room for later sophistication, but the first version should already have sane conservative budgets.
- The scheduler should be a thin policy/orchestration layer on top of the worker, not a second place where backfill correctness, batching semantics, or publish safety are defined.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Tests for scheduler state transitions: idle -> running, busy -> paused, resume after busy period.
- Tests ensuring background semantic work yields to lexical repair and foreground search.
- Performance tests or harnesses validating that background work stays within stated latency/throughput budgets.
- At least one CLI/robot/E2E scenario showing the scheduler driving the worker under load/idle transitions with detailed structured logs.

DONE WHEN:
Semantic backfill can make steady progress in the background on real corpora without materially degrading interactive cass usage or general machine responsiveness, by driving the already-correct worker within conservative operating budgets.","design":"WORKER-FIRST REFINEMENT:
- The scheduler should be able to deliver semantic acquisition/backfill value before the cleanup layer exists, but it should not be the first owner of vector-generation correctness.
- This bead must consume the stable worker/publish contract from bead .8 and the earlier status surface from bead .9, then add only policy: when to run, when to pause, and how to stay within user-friendly budgets.
- The scheduler should enrich the existing readiness/progress surfaces with paused-for-load, resumed-on-idle, and similar policy-specific explanations once it lands.
- Once cleanup is implemented, the scheduler should treat it as an additional budgeted maintenance workload rather than a prerequisite for all background progress.","acceptance_criteria":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- The scheduler runs semantic backfill only under conservative idle or low-pressure conditions, yields promptly when foreground pressure or lexical repair returns, and resumes safely when budgets allow.
- Scheduling decisions are inspectable through logs and status, including why work is running, paused, resumed, disabled, pinned, cooled-down, or budget-blocked, what recent pressure signal triggered the decision, and when work is next eligible to resume, and those decisions honor the cohesive configuration surface without oscillating under borderline load.
- Unit, integration, performance-harness, and CLI or robot E2E scenarios cover idle-to-running, busy-to-paused, resumed-on-idle, lexical-repair preemption, cooldown or anti-thrash behavior, operator overrides, and user-visible budget adherence with preserved traces, timing artifacts, and scheduler-decision evidence.","notes":"TEST POLICY ADDENDUM: This bead must land with bead-local validation, not just final-epic validation. At minimum include targeted unit tests for the new invariants, integration tests for the affected runtime path, and at least one CLI/robot/E2E script when the behavior is user-visible or multi-step. Test output must include rich, structured, timestamped logging and artifact snapshots sufficient for a future agent to diagnose failures without rerunning the entire scenario manually.  {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.8","title":"Implement resumable semantic backfill worker and atomic publish pipeline","description":"BACKGROUND:
A scheduler without a durable worker/publisher only decides when work should happen; it does not actually converge the corpus. cass needs a semantic worker that can process backlog incrementally, checkpoint progress, publish safe artifacts, and resume after interruption without wasting large amounts of prior work. That worker is foundational infrastructure: it should exist before background policy decides exactly when it runs.

GOAL:
Build the semantic worker/publisher that executes the backlog described by bead .5 and can be invoked by later scheduler/orchestration layers without redefining its core correctness model.

SCOPE:
- Consume semantic backlog in bounded batches that can be paused and resumed.
- Build/update fast-tier and quality-tier vector assets incrementally where possible.
- Publish assets atomically so readers never observe partially-written indexes as ready.
- Support crash-safe restart after interruption, process exit, or machine reboot.
- Ensure the worker coexists safely with foreground search, lexical repair, and any ANN acceleration rebuilds.
- Record enough progress metadata that operators can tell where the worker left off.
- Expose a stable worker interface or invocation contract that the scheduler bead can drive later, without forcing scheduling policy to be baked into the worker itself.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- Progress checkpoints should be frequent enough to avoid expensive replay, but not so frequent they dominate IO.
- Publish semantics must align with the state manifests so readiness never races ahead of artifact durability.
- Worker batching should be chosen with future background throttling in mind, not just peak throughput.
- This bead should deliver first semantic-generation correctness even before the background scheduler is complete.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Integration tests for worker interruption and resume.
- Tests for atomic publish behavior under simulated crash windows.
- Tests for mixed readiness states, e.g. fast tier published while quality tier continues.
- At least one CLI/robot/E2E scenario that exercises explicit or manually triggered worker progress with detailed structured logs.

DONE WHEN:

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-ibuuh.9","title":"Expose truthful lexical-vs-semantic readiness, fallback, and progress surfaces","description":"BACKGROUND:
Once cass starts self-healing lexical assets and opportunistically backfilling semantic assets, status reporting becomes more important, not less. Agents and humans need to know whether they are seeing lexical-only results, hybrid-refined results, or a system that is still converging in the background. Ambiguous health output will create repeated confusion and unnecessary repair attempts. Core truth about lexical readiness, semantic readiness, fallback, and active work should not be delayed until the idle/background scheduler layer is complete.

GOAL:
Make status, health, capabilities, and search metadata explicitly describe lexical readiness, semantic readiness, active fallback, and core background-progress truth.

SCOPE:
- Update status/health output so lexical mandatory readiness and semantic optional readiness are reported separately.
- Surface whether a search completed lexically-only, fast-tier-refined, or fully hybrid-refined.
- Report semantic backlog/progress in machine-readable form from the repair/acquisition/worker path, even before scheduler-specific idle/load policy is finished.
- Make recommended actions specific: e.g. repair lexical now, wait for semantic catch-up, or nothing required.
- Ensure CLI/TUI/robot consumers all have access to consistent truth, not three separate ad hoc summaries.
- Leave room for the scheduler bead to enrich the same surfaces later with paused-for-load, resumed-on-idle, and similar policy-specific explanations.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS:
- A stale-but-complete lexical index is different from a missing lexical index.
- A missing quality tier is different from a missing fast tier.
- Metadata should help agents reason about result quality without forcing them to read logs.
- Avoid scaring users with "unhealthy" when ordinary search remains correct and available; reserve strong health failures for blocking lexical issues.

TEST/VALIDATION REQUIREMENTS:
- Tests for status/health JSON payload shape across lexical-ready, lexical-repairing, semantic-backfilling, and hybrid-ready states.
- Tests proving search metadata truthfully describes fallback and refinement level.
- Tests that recommended_action output matches the actual remediation path.
- At least one CLI/robot/E2E scenario showing truthful status before the idle scheduler exists and then after scheduler-specific enrichments land.

DONE WHEN:

 {"id":"coding_agent_session_search-uile","title":"Opt 3.1: Binary Metadata Serialization (50-70% storage reduction)","description":"# Optimization 3.1: Binary Metadata Serialization (50-70% storage reduction)

## Summary
Metadata is stored as JSON text in SQLite, which is verbose and slow to parse.
Binary formats like MessagePack offer 50-70% storage reduction and faster
serialization while maintaining schema evolution support.

## Location
- **File:** src/storage/sqlite.rs
- **Lines:** Schema definition, metadata storage, ConversationMetadata struct
- **Related:** Message storage, search result hydration

## Current State
\`\`\`rust
// Stored as JSON text in SQLite TEXT column
let json = serde_json::to_string(&metadata)?;
conn.execute("INSERT INTO messages (metadata, ...) VALUES (?)", [json])?;

// Reading
let json: String = row.get("metadata")?;
let metadata: ConversationMetadata = serde_json::from_str(&json)?;
\`\`\`

## Problem Analysis
1. **Verbose storage:** JSON field names repeated per row (30-50% overhead)
2. **Slow parsing:** Text parsing slower than binary decode
3. **Size overhead:** Numbers as text, Unicode escaping, whitespace
4. **Index bloat:** Larger column values increase B-tree size

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Binary Format Selection: MessagePack (Recommended)
\`\`\`rust
// Cargo.toml
// rmp-serde = "1"

use rmp_serde as rmps;

// Schema change: metadata column to BLOB, add version byte
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct VersionedMetadata {
    #[serde(rename = "v")]
    version: u8,
    #[serde(flatten)]
    data: ConversationMetadata,
}

const METADATA_FORMAT_VERSION: u8 = 1;

fn serialize_metadata(metadata: &ConversationMetadata) -> Result<Vec<u8>> {
    let versioned = VersionedMetadata {
        version: METADATA_FORMAT_VERSION,
        data: metadata.clone(),
    };
    rmps::to_vec(&versioned).map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("msgpack encode: {}", e))
}

fn deserialize_metadata(bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<ConversationMetadata> {
    if bytes.is_empty() {
        return Err(anyhow::anyhow!("empty metadata"));
    }
    
    // Version check for future format migrations
    let versioned: VersionedMetadata = rmps::from_slice(bytes)
        .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("msgpack decode: {}", e))?;
    
    if versioned.version != METADATA_FORMAT_VERSION {
        // Handle future version migrations here
        tracing::warn!(
            found = versioned.version,
            expected = METADATA_FORMAT_VERSION,
            "Metadata version mismatch"
        );
    }
    
    Ok(versioned.data)
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Dual-Read During Migration
\`\`\`rust
fn read_metadata_compat(row: &Row) -> Result<ConversationMetadata> {
    // Try binary column first (new format)
    if let Ok(bytes) = row.get::<_, Vec<u8>>("metadata_bin") {
        return deserialize_metadata(&bytes);
    }
    
    // Fall back to JSON column (old format)
    if let Ok(json) = row.get::<_, String>("metadata") {
        return serde_json::from_str(&json)
            .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!("json decode: {}", e));
    }
    
    Err(anyhow::anyhow!("no metadata found"))
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Schema Migration Strategy
\`\`\`sql
-- Migration v6 -> v7: Add binary metadata column
-- Phase 1: Add new column alongside old
ALTER TABLE messages ADD COLUMN metadata_bin BLOB;

-- Phase 2: Batch migration (in Rust)
-- SELECT rowid, metadata FROM messages WHERE metadata_bin IS NULL LIMIT 1000;
-- For each row: convert JSON -> binary, UPDATE ... SET metadata_bin = ?

-- Phase 3: After all data migrated, make binary primary
-- (Optional) DROP COLUMN metadata; -- if supported

-- Index on new column if needed for queries
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_messages_has_binary 
    ON messages(rowid) WHERE metadata_bin IS NOT NULL;
\`\`\`

\`\`\`rust
/// Batch migration function
pub fn migrate_metadata_to_binary(conn: &Connection) -> Result<usize> {
    let batch_size = 1000;
    let mut total_migrated = 0;
    
    loop {
        let mut stmt = conn.prepare(
            "SELECT rowid, metadata FROM messages 
             WHERE metadata IS NOT NULL AND metadata_bin IS NULL 
             LIMIT ?"
        )?;
        
        let rows: Vec<(i64, String)> = stmt
            .query_map([batch_size], |row| {
                Ok((row.get(0)?, row.get(1)?))
            })?
            .filter_map(|r| r.ok())
            .collect();
        
        if rows.is_empty() {
            break;
        }
        
        let tx = conn.transaction()?;
        for (rowid, json) in &rows {
            let metadata: ConversationMetadata = serde_json::from_str(json)?;
            let binary = serialize_metadata(&metadata)?;
            tx.execute(
                "UPDATE messages SET metadata_bin = ? WHERE rowid = ?",
                rusqlite::params![binary, rowid],
            )?;
        }
        tx.commit()?;
        
        total_migrated += rows.len();
        tracing::info!(migrated = rows.len(), total = total_migrated, "Migrating metadata");
    }
    
    Ok(total_migrated)
}
\`\`\`

## Implementation Steps
1. [ ] Add rmp-serde to Cargo.toml
2. [ ] Create VersionedMetadata wrapper struct
3. [ ] Implement serialize/deserialize functions
4. [ ] Add migration v6 -> v7 with new column
5. [ ] Implement batch migration function
6. [ ] Update write path to use binary format
7. [ ] Update read path with dual-column support
8. [ ] Benchmark storage size and parse speed
9. [ ] Add JSON export for debugging/troubleshooting

## Comprehensive Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests
\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    
    fn sample_metadata() -> ConversationMetadata {
        ConversationMetadata {
            agent: "claude".to_string(),
            workspace: "/home/user/project".to_string(),
            created_at: 1704067200000,
            session_id: Some("abc123".to_string()),
            tags: vec!["rust".to_string(), "optimization".to_string()],
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }
    
    /// Roundtrip serialization preserves data
    #[test]
    fn test_roundtrip() {
        let original = sample_metadata();
        let bytes = serialize_metadata(&original).unwrap();
        let recovered = deserialize_metadata(&bytes).unwrap();
        
        assert_eq!(original.agent, recovered.agent);
        assert_eq!(original.workspace, recovered.workspace);
        assert_eq!(original.created_at, recovered.created_at);
        assert_eq!(original.session_id, recovered.session_id);
        assert_eq!(original.tags, recovered.tags);
    }
    
    /// Binary format is smaller than JSON
    #[test]
    fn test_size_reduction() {
        let metadata = sample_metadata();
        
        let json = serde_json::to_string(&metadata).unwrap();
        let binary = serialize_metadata(&metadata).unwrap();
        
        let reduction = (json.len() as f64 - binary.len() as f64) / json.len() as f64 * 100.0;
        
        println!("JSON size: {} bytes", json.len());
        println!("Binary size: {} bytes", binary.len());
        println!("Reduction: {:.1}%", reduction);
        
        assert!(binary.len() < json.len(), "Binary should be smaller");
        assert!(reduction >= 30.0, "Should achieve at least 30% reduction");
    }
    
    /// Empty metadata roundtrips correctly
    #[test]
    fn test_empty_metadata() {
        let original = ConversationMetadata::default();
        let bytes = serialize_metadata(&original).unwrap();
        let recovered = deserialize_metadata(&bytes).unwrap();
        
        assert_eq!(original, recovered);
    }
    
    /// Large metadata (many tags)
    #[test]
    fn test_large_metadata() {
        let mut metadata = sample_metadata();
        metadata.tags = (0..100).map(|i| format!("tag_{}", i)).collect();
        
        let bytes = serialize_metadata(&metadata).unwrap();

/// Verify dual-read from both formats
#[test]
fn test_dual_read_compatibility() {
    let conn = Connection::open_in_memory().unwrap();
    
    // Create table with both columns
    conn.execute_batch(
        "CREATE TABLE messages (
            rowid INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            metadata TEXT,
            metadata_bin BLOB
        )"
    ).unwrap();
    
    let metadata = sample_metadata();
    let json = serde_json::to_string(&metadata).unwrap();
    let binary = serialize_metadata(&metadata).unwrap();
    
    // Insert JSON-only row
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT INTO messages (metadata) VALUES (?)",
        [&json],
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Insert binary-only row
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT INTO messages (metadata_bin) VALUES (?)",
        [&binary],
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Insert both columns
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT INTO messages (metadata, metadata_bin) VALUES (?, ?)",
        rusqlite::params![&json, &binary],
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Read all three and verify
    let mut stmt = conn.prepare("SELECT * FROM messages").unwrap();
    let rows: Vec<_> = stmt.query_map([], |row| {
        Ok(read_metadata_compat(row).unwrap())
    }).unwrap().collect();
    
    assert_eq!(rows.len(), 3);
    for recovered in rows {
        let recovered = recovered.unwrap();
        assert_eq!(recovered.agent, metadata.agent);
    }
}

fn test_batch_migration() {

        "CREATE TABLE messages (

            metadata TEXT,
            metadata_bin BLOB
        )"
    ).unwrap();
    
    for i in 0..100 {
        let mut metadata = sample_metadata();
        metadata.session_id = Some(format!("session_{}", i));
        let json = serde_json::to_string(&metadata).unwrap();
        conn.execute("INSERT INTO messages (metadata) VALUES (?)", [&json]).unwrap();
    }
    
    // Run migration
    let migrated = migrate_metadata_to_binary(&conn).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(migrated, 100);
    
    // Verify all rows have binary
    let count: i64 = conn.query_row(
        "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM messages WHERE metadata_bin IS NOT NULL",
        [],
        |r| r.get(0),
    ).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(count, 100);
    
    // Verify data integrity
    let mut stmt = conn.prepare("SELECT metadata_bin FROM messages").unwrap();
    for row in stmt.query_map([], |r| r.get::<_, Vec<u8>>(0)).unwrap() {
        let bytes = row.unwrap();
        let metadata = deserialize_metadata(&bytes).unwrap();
        assert!(!metadata.agent.is_empty());
    }
}

    });

    });

}

/// Full E2E with real database
#[test]
#[ignore] // Run with --include-ignored
fn test_e2e_binary_metadata() {
    use tempfile::TempDir;
    
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let db_path = temp.path().join("test.db");
    
    // Initialize database with schema v7
    let mut storage = SqliteStorage::open(&db_path).unwrap();
    
    // Insert messages with binary metadata
    for i in 0..100 {
        let mut metadata = sample_metadata();
        metadata.session_id = Some(format!("session_{}", i));
        storage.insert_message("test content", &metadata).unwrap();
    }
    
    // Close and reopen
    drop(storage);
    let storage = SqliteStorage::open(&db_path).unwrap();
    
    // Query and verify
    let results = storage.query_all().unwrap();
    assert_eq!(results.len(), 100);
    
    for (i, msg) in results.iter().enumerate() {
        assert_eq!(
            msg.metadata.session_id,
            Some(format!("session_{}", i))
        );
    }
    
    // Verify storage size is reduced
    let file_size = std::fs::metadata(&db_path).unwrap().len();
    println!("Database size: {} KB", file_size / 1024);
}

fn serialize_metadata(metadata: &ConversationMetadata) -> Result<Vec<u8>> {
    let start = std::time::Instant::now();
    let versioned = VersionedMetadata {
        version: METADATA_FORMAT_VERSION,
        data: metadata.clone(),
    };
    let bytes = rmps::to_vec(&versioned)?;
    
    tracing::trace!(
        size = bytes.len(),
        elapsed_us = start.elapsed().as_micros(),
        "Serialized metadata to binary"
    );
    
    Ok(bytes)
}

**Parent Epic:** coding_agent_session_search-zv6w

## Goal

Build the browser-based viewer that authenticates users, decrypts the payload, loads the SQLite database, and provides search + conversation browsing functionality.

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Browser Runtime                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │
│ │ AuthModule    │ │ CryptoModule  │ │ DatabaseModule      │ │
│ │               │ │               │ │                     │ │
│ │ - Password UI │ │ - Argon2 WASM │ │ - sqlite-wasm       │ │
│ │ - QR scanner  │ │ - AES-GCM     │ │ - FTS5 queries      │ │
│ │ - Session mgmt│ │ - Key storage │ │ - Result rendering  │ │
│ └───────────────┘ └───────────────┘ └─────────────────────┘ │
│ ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │
│ │ SearchUI      │ │ ConversationUI│ │ ExportUI            │ │
│ │               │ │               │ │                     │ │
│ │ - Query input │ │ - Message list│ │ - Copy/download     │ │
│ │ - Filters     │ │ - Syntax hl   │ │ - Share links       │ │
│ │ - Results     │ │ - Navigation  │ │ - Print view        │ │
│ └───────────────┘ └───────────────┘ └─────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Worker Architecture

All expensive operations run in a dedicated Web Worker:

```
main thread:                    crypto_worker.js:
  - Auth UI                       - Argon2id derivation
  - Progress display              - DEK unwrapping
  - Rendering                     - Chunk decrypt
                                  - Streaming decompress
                                  - OPFS write
                                  - sqlite-wasm init
```

## Key Technologies

| Library | Purpose | Size (gzip) |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| sqlite-wasm | SQLite in browser (OPFS) | 340KB |
| argon2-browser | Password hashing | 78KB |
| fflate | Streaming decompression | 9KB |
| Marked.js | Markdown rendering | 18KB |
| Prism.js | Syntax highlighting | 11KB |
| DOMPurify | XSS sanitization | 8KB |
| html5-qrcode | QR code scanning | 52KB |

## CSP-Safe UI

No Alpine.js or eval-dependent frameworks. Custom UI layer with:
- No inline event handlers
- No eval() or new Function()
- External CSS only (no inline styles)
- ES modules with proper imports

## File Structure (Assets)

```
src/pages_assets/
├── index.html          # Entry point with CSP meta tag
├── auth.js             # Authentication UI
├── crypto_worker.js    # Decryption worker
├── viewer.js           # Main application
├── search.js           # Search UI component
├── conversation.js     # Conversation renderer
├── styles.css          # Tailwind-based styles
└── vendor/
    ├── sqlite3.js      # sqlite-wasm loader
    ├── sqlite3.wasm
    ├── argon2-wasm.js
    ├── argon2-wasm.wasm
    ├── fflate.min.js
    └── ...
```

| Edge | 102+ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
